Best Practices for Managing a Diverse and Inclusive Social Media Community
==========================================================================

Sep 15, 2023

|

By 

[Aisha Jones](https://www.kubbco.com/blog/best-practices-for-managing-a-diverse-and-inclusive-social-media-community#author)

![Image 6](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/64ac08fa304942f11983252c/65048164d32707701e19883f_image-39.png)

Social media has revolutionized the way we connect and communicate with one another. Platforms like Facebook, Twitter, and Instagram have become our go-to sources for news, entertainment, and social interaction.However, with such immense popularity and an ever-growing user base, social media platforms have also become a space for diverse and complex communities to form and interact. It is crucial for [**social media managers to understand the importance of diversity and inclusion in these communities and to have the skills to manage them effectively**](https://www.kubbco.com/top-10-community-management-tools-for-effective-social-media-engagement/).In this article, we will explore best practices for [managing a diverse and inclusive social media community](https://www.kubbco.com/mastering-the-art-of-community-management-on-tiktok/).

Understanding the Importance of Diversity and Inclusion in Social Media
-----------------------------------------------------------------------

Diversity and inclusion are integral to creating a welcoming and safe environment for everyone in a social media community. Diversity refers to the many different perspectives, experiences, and identities that make up a community, while inclusion refers to the processes and practices that ensure everyone feels respected, valued, and heard.By embracing diversity and promoting inclusivity, social media communities can foster a sense of belonging among their members, which leads to greater engagement and participation.When social media communities are diverse and inclusive, they benefit in many ways. Firstly, diversity can lead to more creativity and innovation, as members from different backgrounds and experiences bring unique ideas and perspectives.This can help the community to grow and develop in new and exciting ways.Secondly, inclusion can increase the sense of community and belonging among members. When everyone feels valued and respected, they are more likely to participate in discussions and share their thoughts and ideas.This can lead to a more engaged and active community. Lastly, diversity and inclusion can help combat discrimination, prejudice, and hate speech. By promoting a culture of respect and inclusivity, social media communities can create a safe space for everyone.

### The Benefits of a Diverse and Inclusive Community

Research has shown that diversity and inclusion can have a positive impact on [**social media communities**](https://www.kubbco.com/the-role-of-empathy-in-building-a-thriving-social-media-community/) in several ways.For example, a study by [**McKinsey & Company**](https://www.mckinsey.com/) found that companies with diverse workforces are more likely to outperform their peers financially. This is because diverse teams are better able to understand and serve a diverse customer base.Similarly, a study by [**Deloitte**](https://www2.deloitte.com/us/en.html) found that inclusive teams are more likely to make better business decisions. This is because inclusive teams are more likely to consider a range of perspectives and ideas before making a decision.Another benefit of a diverse and inclusive community is that it can improve mental health and well-being. When people feel like they belong and are valued, they are more likely to feel happy and fulfilled.This can lead to improved mental health and well-being, which can have a positive impact on all areas of life. Additionally, a diverse and inclusive community can help to reduce feelings of isolation and loneliness.This is particularly important for people who may feel marginalized or excluded from other communities.

### Addressing Common Challenges in Managing Diverse Communities

[**Managing a diverse community**](https://www.kubbco.com/identifying-and-nurturing-superfans-the-key-to-long-term-community-success/) can be challenging. Social media managers need to be aware of the potential barriers and challenges that arise when dealing with different perspectives and opinions, and have the necessary tools and techniques to navigate them effectively.One common challenge is language barriers. Communication can be difficult when members speak different languages. Social media managers can overcome this by providing translation services or encouraging members to use translation tools such as Google Translate.Another challenge is cultural differences. Different cultures have different norms and values, and what may be acceptable in one culture may not be in another. Social media managers need to be sensitive to these differences and ensure that everyone feels respected and valued.Prejudice and discrimination are also common challenges in managing diverse communities. Unfortunately, social media platforms have become a forum for hate speech and discrimination.Managers need to have clear policies in place to address these issues and ensure that members feel safe and protected. This may involve moderating content, removing offensive posts, or banning users who engage in discriminatory behavior.In conclusion, diversity and inclusion are essential for creating a welcoming and safe environment in social media communities. By embracing diversity and promoting inclusivity, social media managers can create a sense of belonging among members, foster creativity and innovation, and combat discrimination and hate speech.While managing a diverse community can be challenging, with the right tools and techniques, social media managers can create a thriving and inclusive community that benefits everyone.

Establishing Community Guidelines and Policies
----------------------------------------------

A key component of managing a diverse and inclusive social media community is to establish clear guidelines and policies that everyone can adhere to. These guidelines should be inclusive and respectful, and should set clear expectations for community members' behavior. Here are some best practices for creating community guidelines:

### Creating Clear and Inclusive Guidelines

It is essential that community guidelines are clear, concise, and easy to understand. Avoid complex language, and use examples to illustrate the kind of behavior that is expected. Guidelines should also be inclusive and incorporate the values of diversity and inclusion.Be sure to address common issues, such as hate speech, harassment, and discrimination, and provide examples of what is and is not acceptable.For example, if your social media community is focused on promoting mental health, your guidelines might include a ban on language that stigmatizes mental illness, such as using the word "crazy" or "psycho" to describe someone.You might also prohibit posts that promote harmful or unproven treatments for mental health issues, such as encouraging people to stop taking medication without consulting a doctor.In addition to outlining what is not acceptable, your guidelines should also provide guidance on what is encouraged. For example, you might encourage members to share their own experiences with mental health, or to offer support and resources to others in the community.

### Enforcing Policies Consistently and Fairly

Once the guidelines are in place, social media managers need to enforce them consistently and fairly. This means applying the same standards across all members, regardless of their identity or background. When a policy violation occurs, managers should follow established procedures, such as issuing warnings or suspending accounts.It is also important to prioritize transparency and accountability, so members can see that policies are enforced fairly and justly.One way to ensure consistency is to have a team of moderators who are trained to enforce the guidelines. These moderators should be familiar with the guidelines and the procedures for enforcing them, and should be able to handle violations in a calm and professional manner.It is also important to listen to feedback from the community and to make adjustments to the guidelines as needed. For example, if members are consistently reporting a certain type of behavior that is not explicitly addressed in the guidelines, it may be necessary to update the guidelines to address that behavior.Overall, establishing clear and inclusive community guidelines is essential for creating a positive and welcoming social media community. By enforcing these guidelines consistently and fairly, social media managers can ensure that all members feel safe and respected.

Encouraging Positive and Respectful Interactions
------------------------------------------------

Another crucial aspect of managing a diverse and inclusive social media community is to promote positive and respectful interactions among members. Social media managers should actively encourage empathy, understanding, and open-mindedness, which can help foster a sense of community and belonging.Here are some best practices for encouraging positive interactions:

### Promoting Empathy and Understanding Among Community Members

Empathy and understanding are essential for creating a safe and inclusive community. Social media managers can encourage these values by sharing resources on how to better understand and appreciate different perspectives and experiences.This could include articles, podcasts, or videos that showcase diverse voices and experiences, as well as hosting events and discussions that promote dialogue and mutual understanding.It's important for community members to understand that everyone has different life experiences and backgrounds that shape their perspectives. Encouraging empathy means acknowledging and respecting those differences and striving to understand where others are coming from.One way to promote empathy is by sharing stories of people who have overcome adversity or faced challenges. These stories can inspire others to be more understanding and compassionate towards those who may be struggling.

### Addressing and Resolving Conflicts Effectively

Conflict is inevitable in any community, and managers need to have the skills and tools to address and resolve it effectively. They should create a space where members feel comfortable sharing their concerns and grievances and provide support to those who are affected.Managers should also be able to de-escalate conflicts and find mutually beneficial solutions that promote harmony and understanding.Effective conflict resolution involves active listening, empathy, and a willingness to compromise. Managers should strive to understand the root cause of a conflict and work towards finding a solution that addresses everyone's concerns.This may involve bringing in a neutral third party or mediator to help facilitate the conversation.It's important for managers to create a culture where conflicts are seen as opportunities for growth and learning. By addressing conflicts in a constructive and respectful manner, managers can help build trust and strengthen relationships within the community.

### Celebrating Diversity and Inclusion

Finally, social media managers should actively celebrate diversity and inclusion within their community. This can involve highlighting different cultures, traditions, and perspectives, as well as recognizing and celebrating important events and milestones.By celebrating diversity, managers can help create a sense of belonging and pride among community members. This can help foster a culture of respect and appreciation for different backgrounds and experiences.Overall, promoting positive and respectful interactions within a diverse social media community requires a proactive and intentional approach. By promoting empathy, addressing conflicts effectively, and celebrating diversity and inclusion, managers can help create a safe and welcoming space for all members.

Fostering Representation and Inclusivity in Content
---------------------------------------------------

The content of a social media community is a reflection of its values and priorities. To create a diverse and inclusive community, managers should ensure that their content is representative and inclusive of all members. Here are some best practices for fostering representation and inclusivity in content:

### Showcasing Diverse Voices and Perspectives

By showcasing diverse voices and perspectives, social media managers can create a community that is inclusive and celebratory of diversity. This means featuring content that represents different races, ethnicities, genders, sexual orientations, religions, and abilities.Managers can do this by featuring guest contributors, highlighting member stories and achievements, and sharing content that represents different viewpoints and experiences.For example, a social media community focused on mental health could feature stories from individuals with different mental health conditions, including those that are often stigmatized or underrepresented.This could include stories from individuals with schizophrenia, bipolar disorder, or borderline personality disorder. By showcasing these diverse perspectives, the community can become a safe and welcoming space for all members.

### Ensuring Accessibility for All Community Members

Accessibility is crucial for ensuring that all members can fully participate in the community. Managers should ensure that their content is accessible to members with disabilities, such as providing audio descriptions for videos, using alt text for images, and creating captions for audio content.Accessibility also includes ensuring that the community platform is usable and navigable for members with disabilities.For example, a social media community focused on fitness could ensure that their videos are accessible to members with visual impairments by providing audio descriptions of the exercises being performed.They could also provide captions for audio content for members who are deaf or hard of hearing. By ensuring that their content is accessible, the community can become more welcoming and inclusive for all members.

### Creating Safe Spaces for Underrepresented Groups

In addition to showcasing diverse voices and ensuring accessibility, social media managers can create safe spaces for underrepresented groups within the community. This means creating content and events that are specifically tailored to the needs and interests of these groups.For example, a social media community focused on parenting could create a subgroup for single parents, providing a safe space for them to connect and share their experiences.Similarly, a community focused on entrepreneurship could create a subgroup for women entrepreneurs, providing a supportive space for them to network and share resources.By creating these safe spaces, social media managers can ensure that all members feel welcomed and valued within the community. This can lead to increased engagement and a stronger sense of community among all members.

### Conclusion

Fostering representation and inclusivity in content is crucial for creating a diverse and welcoming social media community. By showcasing diverse voices and perspectives, ensuring accessibility, and creating safe spaces for underrepresented groups, social media managers can create a community that is inclusive and celebratory of diversity.

Monitoring and Assessing Community Engagement
---------------------------------------------

Finally, social media managers need to continuously assess the effectiveness of their diversity and inclusion strategies and make adjustments as needed.This means monitoring key metrics such as engagement, participation, and sentiment to determine if members feel valued and included. Here are some best practices for monitoring and assessing community engagement:

### Tracking Key Metrics to Measure Diversity and Inclusion

Managers should develop a set of key performance indicators (KPIs) that measure the community's diversity and inclusion performance. These metrics could include the number of diverse voices featured, the incidence of hate speech and harassment, and the diversity of the community's audience.

### Adjusting Strategies Based on Community Feedback and Data

Community feedback and data can provide valuable insights into how well the community is performing in terms of diversity and inclusion. Managers should use this feedback to make informed decisions and adjust their strategies accordingly.This could involve tweaking the community guidelines, investing in accessibility features, or providing more resources to promote diversity and inclusion.

Conclusion
----------

In conclusion, managing a diverse and inclusive social media community is a complex but rewarding challenge. By embracing the values of diversity and inclusion, social media managers can create a community that is welcoming, supportive, and empowering.It takes effort, skill, and commitment to create an environment where all members feel valued and heard, but the benefits are well worth it. By following the best practices outlined in this article, you can become an effective and responsible social media manager who fosters a diverse and inclusive community.

![](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/61a75b69dd6ac4387ca3f748/6855ac35aca8a67a7d6221e3_Feminuity%20%5BF%5D%20Logo%20Dark%20(3).png)
![Woman with hearing aid using iPad](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/61a75b69dd6ac42093a3f754/621e6d047e35c1da1049a6a1_61f9c7a4f451870784826f01_Woman%20with%20hearing%20aid%20using%20iPad.png)

# The Future is Inclusive, Accessible, and Digital

This guide is a collection of our learnings about inclusive and accessible social media engagement and leading practices for digital engagement.

**â**

## **Why Inclusive and Accessible Social Media Practices?**

Social media is an essential tool for communication. When used properly, it can provide a range of users with necessary, engaging, and topical information about our brands and the world around us. However, when inclusion and accessibility are not at the forefront, it can exacerbate inequity and cause barriers to inclusion.

Diversity, equity, and inclusion live in how we create and develop materials and the way we post and format them to the platforms we use. Inclusive design must be at the foundation of our social media practices. Inclusive design accounts for a range of diversityâincluding ability, language, gender, age, and other factorsâby creating a variety of ways for people to engage. This guide is a collection of our learnings to date regarding inclusive and accessible social media engagement, along with some leading practices for digital engagement.

Overall, keeping social media accessible and inclusive means recognizing exclusion, learning from your (potential) followers, and presenting information in the clearest ways possible.

## **Tips for Written Content**

### Inclusive Language

How we write and engage with followers should not do more harm than good. When writing captions for photos to accompany articles or as standalone posts, proofread any written content through the lens of equity, inclusion, and accessibility. The following are some key points to keep in mind.

#### Use The Correct Pronouns**Â**

If using pronouns to refer to someone, make sure you are using their correct pronouns. Do not assume someoneâs gender pronouns. Instead, ask for them. And if you cannot ask, use their name.

#### Learn The Basics**Â**

Use inclusive language throughout posts. If unsure if the language you are using is inclusive or appropriate, check out our [Inclusive Language Guide](https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/1TTQzwjIT5VQXg_iKPMu9abhZ4b4SoGAOdxlC-QrSd4c/edit#slide=id.gbb3f17905a_0_38).

#### Be Strategic with #'s and @'s

Add hashtags and mentions at the end of your captions. Punctuation marks are read aloud by screen readers, so hashtags or @ mentions can disrupt copy.

#### Avoid JargonÂ

If using technical terms for your specific field or industry, do not assume everyone is familiar with the language. Explain jargon, scientific, legal, and economic terms. Learn about harmful language that may be in your industry by reading "[Language & Inclusion: An Analysis Across Industries](https://www.feminuity.org/blog/language-inclusion-an-analysis-across-industries)" on our blog.

#### Donât Make Assumptions

Avoid using culturally specific references as much as possible. Some people may not understand or relate, leading to a sense of exclusion.

#### **Black Vernacular English**

If you are non-Black, avoid using Black Vernacular English (BVE) when writing captions or adding GIFs. While often used to seem ârelatable,â words such as âlit,â âwoke,â âbae,â âratchet,â âsis,â âslay, âhella,â or âbasic,â and phrases such as âstraight up,â âon fleek,â âI feel you,â or âturn up,â are common sayings that are often misused or overly emphasized.Â

When BVE is used by non-Black people, it erases its origin and history while commodifying parts of Black culture. Learn more about its history by reading "[Using BVE as a Non-Black Person Is Appropriation](https://www.feminuity.org/blog/using-bve-as-a-non-black-person-is-appropriation)" on our blog.

### Content or Trigger Warnings

When posting or sharing content that may be potentially traumatic or set off negative emotions for some people, consider providing a content warning. This can be done in the caption of the article or image or on the first social card on a series of posts.

Do your due diligence to make sure the content warning is the first thing folks engage with before the potentially triggering content. This gives people the power to decide or prepare themselves to engage. The following are some topics that you should include warnings for:

â

### Community Standards

When providing a platform for engagement online, you cannot guarantee the space will be safe for everyone to engage with your content. However, you can put in the effort to set a standard for the types of interactions that are appropriate. One method of doing so is through creating Community Standards.Â

Community standards outline how the account page is committed to creating an inclusive space through how you will engage and expect others to engage. Some ideas to include are as follows.

#### Our Community Standards

## Visual ContentÂ

### Tips for Visual Content

#### Inclusive Images

When using images, try to be authentic about representation. If there is a group photo, does it include a diversity of body size and shape, socialized race, gender expression, religious expression, abilities, etc.? Review your timelines or feeds, and ask yourself critical questions such as, what types of people are represented? Is there one group that is depicted more than others?Â

Too often, brands over-represent young, white, straight, non-disabled, cis-gender men in their imagery. Not only does this often miss the mark, but it can also marginalize those who donât fit that description.

#### Stock Images

Consider using stock images that represent a diversity of experiences. Check out some of these:

â

â

#### Avoid Stereotypes

â

When being intentional about image diversity, avoid using certain groups to represent stereotypical messages. For example, there are limited options when sourcing images of women or racialized people in positions of power. Instead, they are often represented as âsassyâ for comedic purposes. Counter stereotypes in your imagery.

â

#### Ask for Consent

â

If you are using photos of team members, ensure you have their full consent by telling them where and how you're going to use their image.

â

#### Minimize Negative Impact

â

Avoid reproducing unrealistic beauty standards. Consider the role you can play in helping to minimize social media's negative effect on mental health as much as possible.

â

#### Consider MessagingÂ

â

Consider role assignment and portrayal as well. Are women always cleaning? Is romance always heterosexual?

â

#### Colour and Colour Contrast

â

Colour contrast values help make images and text visible. Almost one in seven people have some form of vision impairment, ranging from colour blindness to low vision, near vision, or blindness. In fact, Facebookâs colour scheme is blue because its founder, Mark Zuckerberg, is red-green colourblind.

â

There are specific resources to support you in determining sufficient colour contrast. Aim for a ratio of 4.5:1.

â

â

![A visual showing what colour contrasts are OK ](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/61a75b69dd6ac42093a3f754/61f9cca2735193076775d6a7_Screen%20Shot%202022-02-01%20at%207.13.11%20PM.png)

#### Colour and Symbols

#### Animations

Flashing lights (specifically flashing three times/second) are dangerous and sometimes cause seizures. Donât use flashing lights in the videos, gifs, and animations you produce. Whatever effect you are trying to achieve, you can probably achieve in another way.

#### Links

Visualize links. Add an underline or a hover animation to convey that you can click hyperlinked text.

â

![A visual showing an optimal form of hyperlink text](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/61a75b69dd6ac42093a3f754/61f9cd19836cbb128f6c36c0_Screen%20Shot%202022-02-01%20at%207.15.10%20PM.png)

### Inclusive Emoji Use

â

Emojis are more than the silly, fun visuals we include in our text messages; they are a pervasive means of communication that have taken on symbolic meanings of their own. As such, we must be intentional about using emojis inclusively.Â

â

Take some time to review our "[Quick Guide to Inclusive Emoji Use](https://www.feminuity.org/blog/a-quick-guide-to-inclusive-emoji-use)" on our blog. The following are some high-level points.

â

â

### Inclusive GIF and Meme Use

â

When using GIFs and Memes to enhance a social media post, it is important not to do more harm than good. Because anyone can make GIFs, it is important to select a GIF or meme through a DEI lens. GIFs and memes can cause harm if they perpetuate stereotypes, reinforce discrimination, and/or mock marginalized communities. Inclusive representation and language are important when you choose to use GIFs or memes.

â

#### **BVE and GIFs**

â

Black Vernacular English (BVE) is often harmfully used in reaction GIFs. These GIFs often represent Black women as âsassyâ and extravagant while allowing non-Black users to use these images as an expression or image to complement their words. GIFs with transcripts become an opportunity for those not fluent in BVE to use the language, such as âhell no,â âgirl, bye,â and âbitch, please.âÂ

â

Ultimately, these images are relied upon to perform fury, annoyance, shade, or celebratory moments. Representation matters and the continual representation of Black people as an image to vocalize these expressions may reinforce stereotypes while failing to see the terms as more than a âpunch line.â This has also been characterized as "digital blackface," which has ties to the representation of Black people in media and the arts throughout history. Learn more about this phenomenon by reading [Memes and Misogynoir](https://www.theawl.com/2014/08/memes-and-misogynoir/). Explore more about BVE in the Inclusive Language section.

â

### Text and Font

If formatting text on images, you should consider the following key points.

â

## Posting on Social Media

### Alternative Text

â

Alternative (alt) text is an important method for accessible image posting. Alt-text is used to describe your photos for folks with visual needs. Keep the following key points in mind when using alt-text.

#### Learn From The Best

[WebAIM](https://webaim.org/techniques/alttext/#basics) offers tips and good examples, [Phase2](https://www.phase2technology.com/blog/no-more-excuses) provides a number of scenarios, and copywriter [Ashley Bischoff](http://www.friendlyediting.com/assets/altrock/index.html#cover) offers a number of tips.

### How to Add Alt Text to Your Social Media

#### Facebook New Photo

#### Facebook Already Uploaded Photo

#### Twitter

#### LinkedIn

#### Instagram

### Alt Text for Gifs

### Hashtags

You can optimize hashtags in a variety of ways. They can help people find your content and generate new followers. However, this can only happen if everyone can engage with your hashtags.

Every word in a hashtag should be capitalized or in camel case. It is not only easier to engage or read, but screen readers are able to pick up each word individually instead of as one long word, which is undetectable. For example, #GenderEquityAndDiversity is better than #genderequityanddiversity.

#### Hashtags and Social Justice

â

Brands are often quick to support social movements by participating in hashtag campaigns. Think #BlackLivesMatter, #MeToo, #LoveWins, #GirlsLikeUs, and #RefugeesWelcome among many others. While hashtag movements have been proven to make a difference, organizations often monetize social movements without actually backing them.Â

â

People often use hashtags to share resources, personal stories, organize, and show support. When using hashtags that back a wider movement or discuss an important social issue, be mindful that you are contributing constructively and genuinely to the conversation. Do not use these hashtags superficially or in a way that crowds out more meaningful information and testimonies such as the surge of Black tiles that overwhelmed the #BlackLivesMatter hashtag on #BlackOutTuesday. To learn more, [check out how Blackout Tuesday Drowned Out Vital information Shared Under the BLM Hashtag.](https://www.theverge.com/2020/6/2/21277852/blackout-tuesday-posts-hiding-information-blm-black-lives-matter-hashtag)

â

## **Live Captioning**

â

Whenever possible, videos or stories on Instagram and Facebook need to include live captions or subtitles. For those who are Deaf, Hard of Hearing, or who may rely on closed captioning, getting captions can be a lengthy and expensive process. Providing live captioning on your content ensures equity and inclusion in this process. The following are some methods that can support you with this.

â

### Facebook

â

â

### Instagram Reels

### Do You Require Live Captioning?

If you are someone who requires the use of live captioning, check out [Live Captioning for Remote Meetings & More](https://www.feminuity.org/blog/resource-live-captioning-for-remote-meetings-amp-more). It offers two scenarios where you can use speech-to-text apps to help bring live captioning to your next remote meeting or social hangout.Â

## Citing or Crediting Sources

There are so many accounts on social media that are coming up with thoughtful and useful resources, social cards, articles, and posts. Reshare them! But when doing so, be sure to credit them properly and sufficiently for this work.Â

Members of dominant groups often appropriate the ideas and messages of marginalized creators, and as a result, receive praise and recognition for work they did not do. Citing is an essential feminist and equity practice.Â

When doing so, keep the following in mind:

â

## Leveraging Social Media with Intention

Social media can be a useful place for you to affirm your commitment and actions for inclusion, take a stance on important social issues and current events, and provide forums for support. When done well, it can reach wider audiences and support culture-add in your company.

Your social media has the potential to be a productive source of interaction and engagement. And you may mess up along the way - that's completely natural in any learning journey. But when you do mess up, itâs how you make up for it that counts. If you create a caption, image, or post that is not accessible or inclusive - humble yourself. Take the post down andâdepending on the situationâissue a sincere apology. Use it as an opportunity to educate others on accessibility and inclusion.

â

â

## Important Note

â

This resource is not meant to be a static guide, but rather a compilation and reflection of our learnings to date. Everything changes - from technologies and innovations to social norms, cultures, languages, and more. Weâll continue to update this resource with your feedback; email us at [hello@feminuity.org](mailto:hello@feminuity.org) with suggestions.

â

## About The Author

â

This resource was written collaboratively by members of the Feminuity team.

â

## Give Credit Where Credit's Due

â

**If you wish to reference this work, please use the following citation:** Feminuity. "The Future is Inclusive, Accessible, and Digital: A Guide to Accessible and Inclusive Social Media Practices"

--- SOURCE 5: Anti-Racism, Equity and Inclusion Best Practices for Social Media ---
URL: https://www.buffalo.edu/brand/resources-tools/diversity-inclusion-guidelines/social-media.html

SUMMARY:
Anti-Racism, Equity and Inclusion Best Practices for Social Media · 1. Look at the breadth of your content and plan · 2. Be intentional with language · 3.

FULL CONTENT:
# Anti-Racism, Equity and Inclusion Best Practices for Social Media

Social media serves an important role in helping to connect with our communities, both on and off campus, as well as increase awareness and strengthen our university’s reputation. It’s often one of the first places our prospective students go to determine whether they can see themselves at UB. It’s also where many of our current students, employees, alumni and the Buffalo community go to learn about UB events, news and updates.

As social media practitioners, we all share the responsibility to ensure that our communications on these social media platforms are authentic and in alignment with our values of inclusion, diversity, anti-racism and equity so that everyone feels welcome at UB.

The guidance below will help examine some basic ways that we can start to evolve our social media communications by being conscious of the content and visuals we choose to promote on our pages as well as how we present them.

### What to consider:

## 1. Look at the breadth of your content and plan

Social media content should be viewed holistically. Not everyone will feel included or represented in every post, photo, video or story that is published, so it is important that the overall breadth of content be inclusive.

When selecting content, keep in mind that diversity can mean many things, from gender to race or ethnicity to age to experience or background. Social media practitioners should strive to publish a balanced portfolio of stories and visuals that a variety of audiences will be able to relate to.  
   
 As a litmus test, consider looking at the content published from your account(s) for the last week or month and consider whether or not the language, the people and the photos you are featuring are inclusive and depict an accurate representation of your unit and/or our community as a whole.

Consider creating a content plan to make sure that you are regularly featuring diverse populations in your content. Are there national or cultural days that you can incorporate annually into your social calendar that reflect your diverse audience and UB’s values? Is there a monthly or weekly student/club feature that you create that allows you to highlight various members of our community? Think about the content that you are already publishing and how you can make changes to your workflows and process to curate more inclusive content.

## 2. Be intentional with language

There is immense power in language: It can deepen understanding and strengthen relationships, or it can confuse or cause harm. For these reasons, it is critically important to carefully consider every word and phrase as well as the symbols, metaphors and subtext in a piece and where and how they are placed. Imagine how others could interpret or be impacted by what you’re saying, and remember that not everyone has the same experiences with language—inoffensive language to one person may be offensive, demeaning or harmful to another.  
   
 If you’re not sure how your words will affect others, speak with people of different backgrounds, abilities or experiences.

Always remember to refer to UB’s style guide to ensure you are using the most up-to-date inclusive language.

## 3. Consider (and reconsider) context

When selecting visuals, be mindful of what the images may be inadvertently communicating. These images can either contribute to or combat harmful stereotypes and affect the reputation of the university.

For example, images used to talk about financial aid and scholarships should feature students of all races and backgrounds to convey that all students may need—and have access to—financial support.  
   
 Context also includes representations of order and hierarchy. Though evaluating content for this type of context can be extremely nuanced, it is no less important. As an example, many of us as marketers are sensitive to unintended representations of order and hierarchy in stock photography (e.g., a woman at a computer with a man leaning over her shoulder). Always select and review with an eye for such representations.  
   
 ​​For this reason, stock photography should especially be used with sensitivity, as it can read as inauthentic, and can depict these harmful stereotypes and representations.

## 4. Avoid appropriation

Appropriation takes an aspect from a culture and uses it without knowing or honoring the meaning behind it, or respecting the people of a culture. Drawing from people’s cultures, traditions and personal experiences is both subjective and sensitive. While the intention behind using an aspect of a culture may be admiration, if done without respect or awareness, appropriation may perpetuate stereotypes or exploit groups of people.

We can all lead with cultural respect and awareness by being mindful of nuance and historical context, learning and honoring the culture, seeking guidance and diverse opinions, evaluating intent and impact, and elevating authentic voices.

## 5. Make content accessible

To truly be inclusive, social media practitioners must follow accessibility best practices to ensure that the content is available for people of all abilities. While we cannot control what accessibility features are on each social media platform, we can leverage the existing features to optimize our content, by following these best practices:

## 6. Put historical content into context

Using archival footage or photos can be challenging when trying to balance inclusivity as some materials reflect a time when UB was less diverse.

When deciding to use historical content, such as photos, ask yourself what message you are sending by publishing it. If the photo does not reflect diversity, consider:

If reflecting diversity cannot be achieved using these tactics, consider taking a pass on the content.

For further guidance, review [UB’s Historical Content Best Practices](/brand/resources-tools/diversity-inclusion-guidelines/historical-content.html).

## 7. Don’t misrepresent or overuse content

Avoid utilizing photos of students or employees on social media that do not correlate to the content. Images of students and employees should not be used to promote units or departments that they aren’t affiliated with. For example, if you are trying to promote the School of Engineering and Applied Sciences, a photo of art students should not be used because it reflects better diversity. It’s important that our content is authentic and accurately represents our community.  
   
 It may not always be possible to know for certain if you are featuring students, faculty, etc. who are from your unit, but when utilizing shared image repositories like SmugMug, look for context in the photo tags or descriptions to help eliminate misrepresentation. Additionally, when searching for images on SmugMug, do not rely on keywords for diversity. Instead search the appropriate content and use a critical eye to find the image that will work best.  
   
 Additionally, try not to overuse photos on your pages because they convey diversity. It’s acceptable to use the same photo more than once, but if that image is used repeatedly over time, it can have the reverse effect and instead convey a lack of diversity to your audience. Overusing these images commoditizes the people depicted within them and may harm their relationship with the university.  
   
 To find updated photos to use on your social media accounts, sort the SmugMug photo database by “most recent” to view the newest images available. If you need help finding images or are in need of new photography within a specific area, contact University Communications for assistance.

## 8. Understand how to use emojis and symbols

Consider the sensitivity around symbols and emojis that relate to culture, race and gender. Make sure that you understand the meaning of an emoji before you use it to avoid accidentally posting something that is insensitive or inappropriate. If you are unsure if a certain use is appropriate, consult with your [Unit Social Media Lead](/ubsocial/who-we-are/our-circles/smleads.html "Unit Social Media Leads") or University Communications before moving forward.

Additionally, it is important to showcase diversity through the use of emojis. Don’t always use the default emoji shade in your keyboard when utilizing people emojis. Use every shade that is offered as you engage and post your content.

For more information, and considerations to take, when utilizing emojis in your social media content, review UB's Emoji Best Practices.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Promote diversity by fostering environments where all voices are heard. Implement guidelines that embrace empathy, respect, and understanding to create a sense of belonging.
Utilize social media to highlight disparities in representation and access. Share stories and data to educate audiences about systemic issues affecting marginalized communities.
Ensure all community members have equal access to information and tools. Advocate for inclusive digital practices, such as accessible content and varied engagement methods.
